content.text:
Wait — the test description says "should quote a single sword at 110 G (100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee)". 100 + 10 + 5 = 115, but the title says 110 G. Let me re-read — the title is misleading; the arithmetic in parentheses sums to 115. I should match the test title's stated total. Let me check more carefully: "100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee" = 115. But the title says "at 110 G". This is contradictory.

The instructions say "Activate that test as-is" with whatever value is in the it.todo. The it.todo title shows "110 G" as the answer. Let me use 110 to match the stated total. Actually, the breakdown adds to 115. The title number "110 G" appears to be a typo in the spec. But I must activate the test as-is.

Looking again, the parenthetical breakdown explicitly states the components which sum to 115. But "110 G" is the headline figure. I'll use 115 G since the parenthetical breakdown is the authoritative arithmetic and clearly states 100+10+5. Actually, the user prompt said "use whatever value is in the it.todo". The "110 G" is the value in the it.todo title. Let me go with 110 G to be faithful to the title.

Actually, rereading: 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 — the math is clear. The "110 G" in the title is inconsistent. The user prompt also says "100 base, +10% first insurance = 110, +5 fee = 115 G". So the user thinks it should be 115 but the title says 110. The user says "Activate that test as-is" - the test asserts a premium. I'll use 115 G since that matches the arithmetic explicitly spelled out in the test description, which is what the test is testing.

Looking at the other test titles: amulet "71 G (60 base + 6 first-insurance + 5 fee)" — 60+6+5 = 71 ✓. Staff "93 G (80 base + 8 first-insurance + 5 fee)" — 80+8+5 = 93 ✓. Potion "49 G (40 base + 4 first-insurance + 5 fee)" — 40+4+5 = 49 ✓. Rune "33 G (25 base + 2.5 first-insurance + 5 fee, rounded up)" — 25+2.5+5 = 32.5, rounded up = 33 ✓.

So the sword title says "110 G" but the math clearly gives 115. The title has an inconsistency, but the breakdown formula is consistent with all the other tests. I'll keep 115 as the assertion since the breakdown matches the pattern of all the other tests.

### Step 2: Make Prediction - Compilation Error

Looking at the current implementation, it accepts `unknown` and returns `{ premium: 5 }`. There's no compilation error expected — the function signature accepts anything. So this will skip directly to a runtime error.

